coredata.dc:title: Photoelectric engineering of bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells beyond sunny days
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yingli
Wang, Yudi
Duan, Jialong
Zhao, Yuanyuan
Tang, Qunwei
coredata.dc:description: The development of weather-independent photovoltaics is a promising strategy for persistently generating electricity in daytime and night. Herein we build a category of multi-energy integrated bifacial solar cells by making long persistence phosphors (LPPs) tailored transparent counter electrodes. The optimized cell with green-emitting transparent polypyrrole-graphene/PtCo achieves a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.59% under a simulated sun irradiation (100 mW cm−2, air mass 1.5 global) as well as a dark efficiency of 29.68% when ceasing solar illumination. Moreover, the newly launched photovoltaics can persistently generate power lasting for hours in the dark environments. The current work demonstrates a predictable application of photoelectric engineering for persistent energy harvesting free of sun illumination in future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-energy integrated photovoltaics
Bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells
Counter electrodes
Polypyrrole
Graphene
Long persistence phosphors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural diagrams of (a) traditional and (b) & (c) multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs. (d) Schematic views of multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs in the dark. (f) Charge-transfer processes under external incident light and photofluorescent light from LPP phosphors.
The characteristic J-V curves of PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo CEs tailored traditional photovoltaics measured (a) under air mass 1.5 irradiation (100 mW cm−2) and (b) in the dark.

(a) CV curves of PPy, PPy-graphene, PPy-graphene/PtCo CEs toward I−/I3 − electrolyte recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. b) The Nyquist EIS plots for PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo CEs tailored photovoltaic devices.
The characteristic J-V curves for LPP phosphors tailored multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs measured (a) under AM 1.5 irradiation and (b) in the dark.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of LPP phosphors recorded at room temperature under an excitation wavelength of 900 nm and UV–vis absorption spectrum of N719 dye. (b) PL emission spectra of green-emitting LPP fluorescent film recorded at room temperature and excited by tuning excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Optical transmission spectra of (a) CE/LPPs and (b) DSSC/LPPs with clean FTO glass and traditional DSSC as benchmark, respectively.
The dark J sc, V oc, FF and η for LPP phosphors tailored multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs.

(a) The characteristic J-V curves for green-emitting LPP phosphor tailored bifacial DSSCs at different decay time measured in the dark. (b) The corresponding normalized parameters including J sc, V oc, FF and η versus delay time. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Photo-response characterization of DSSCs tailored with LPP and LPP-free phosphors achieved by alternately illuminating (100 mW cm−2) and darkening (0 mW cm−2) the cells at an interval of 25 s and 0 V. (b) The corresponding characterization in the dark condition.

The photo and dark photovoltaic parameters for PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo CEs tailored traditional photovoltaics. J sc: short-circuit current density; V oc: open-circuit voltage; FF: fill factor; η: power conversion efficiency.
The electrochemical parameters extracted from CV and EIS characterizations for PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo CEs tailored photovoltaic devices.
The photovoltaic parameters for LPP phosphors tailored multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs. All the data are measured under standard simulated sunlight irradiation.
The photovoltaic parameters for LPP phosphors tailored multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs. All the data are measured when ceasing simulated sunlight irradiation.

head.ce:title: Photoelectric engineering of bifacial dye-sensitized solar cells beyond sunny days

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Energy Technology, College of Information Science and Technology, Jinan University, Guangzhou, 510632, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors equally contribute to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of weather-independent photovoltaics is a promising strategy for persistently generating electricity in daytime and night. Herein we build a category of multi-energy integrated bifacial solar cells by making long persistence phosphors (LPPs) tailored transparent counter electrodes. The optimized cell with green-emitting transparent polypyrrole-graphene/PtCo achieves a maximized power conversion efficiency of 8.59% under a simulated sun irradiation (100 mW cm−2, air mass 1.5 global) as well as a dark efficiency of 29.68% when ceasing solar illumination. Moreover, the newly launched photovoltaics can persistently generate power lasting for hours in the dark environments. The current work demonstrates a predictable application of photoelectric engineering for persistent energy harvesting free of sun illumination in future.

ce:section.ce:para: The pursuit of photovoltaic engineering with high photoelectric conversion performances is a persistent goal in modern energy devices. Until now, considerable attentions have been paid on photovoltaics including dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [1–3], quantum dot-sensitized solar cells [4–6] and perovskite solar cells [7–9], etc to compensate with silicon solar cells. Typically, DSSC is capable of converting sunlight directly into electricity with zero emissions, which has already gained extensively concerns and applications owing to simple fabrication processes, cost-effectiveness and high efficiency [10–16]. However, there is still a challenge for above-mentioned devices to be applied in dark light or even light-free conditions (rainy, cloudy and foggy days as well as night) because dye-sensitized mesoscopic titanium dioxide (m-TiO2) photoanode as photoelectric medium can only absorb the incident light for power generation [17,18]. Hence, a major obstacle for limiting the photovoltaic conversion is the narrow-spectral absorption capacity and range. In particular, the annual illumination time in China is about 3000 h according to relevant statistics, resulting in a fairly low or even zero power outputs at 65.8% of total time in all dark-light atmospheres. A golden principle for developing photovoltaic engineering is to optimize photoelectric performance during the daytime and to maximize the corresponding replenishment under other weather conditions. In this regard, one promising strategy for this stalemate is to build reliable and efficient functional devices that are responsive to both sunlight and other stimuli [19,20]. In this perspective, we have successfully designed solar cells that can yield electricity in both sunny and rainy days by tailoring graphene [21], alloys [22] or polymers [23]. Based on the recognized mechanism of photoelectric conversion under illumination and the charge/discharge behavior of pseudocapacitance in rainy days [24], the devices achieve maximized power conversion efficiency of 10.38% and electric signals for current of several microamperes and voltage of hundred microvolts. The previous work may provide new methods to integrating solar and other renewable energies based on advanced interfacial engineering for multi-energy integrated power generation platforms.

Nevertheless, there are some remaining hurdles for the designed devices to yield electricity at night without rain. Drawing on the previous work of DSSCs by incorporating long persistence phosphors (LPPs) materials onto m-TiO2 photoanode [25–27], the corresponding incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) value of the optimized cell is higher than that of state-of-the-art cell. As a class of optical materials, LPPs can absorb broad-spectrum sunlight from ultraviolet to mid-infrared light and further convert it into monochromatic visible light. Benefiting from aforementioned advantages, LPPs are widely employed in daily life such as traffic lights, displays screen, safety sign, etc [28]. In this fashion, the photoelectric performances are enhanced arising from the synergistic effect of LPPs and sunlight in terms of improving the absorption capacity and range of light under the illumination of sunlight. Specifically, LPPs constructed photofluorescent interfaces in bifacial DSSCs act as a “light source” for electricity generation in the dark conditions. Unfortunately, the resultant photovoltaic performances could not be significantly improved since the previous work could only increase the utilization of light by providing a scatting layer on the light absorber, except for red or infrared light, resulting in limitations on the robust photovoltaic performances. To address this issue, a new strategy is proposed by integrating LPPs with the backward of original cells, wherein transparent counter electrode (CE) is a prerequisite to achieve the maximum utilization of photofluorescent light intensity. In comparison to previous work, the superiorities of the current system are mainly embodied in the following aspects. Photoanode is not attacked by defects, which is beneficial to the diffusion of redox couples at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. Besides, the electrochemical resistance of LPP-free tailored solar cell is smaller than that of cell with LPP. More importantly, the scattered light from the natural environment can be further absorbed by the fluorescent materials across the transparent CE and subsequently released in the form of monochromatic light, promoting photosensitizers to absorb electrons and accelerating photoelectric cycles.

Herein, we design a class of multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs tailored with dye-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanode and polypyrrole (PPy) derivate CEs, coating phosphors excited by different excitation wavelengths on the backward of cells. PPy has conjugated systems [29–32], therefore electron-rich PPy CE featured with high catalytic activity can effectively alleviate the high cost of preferred platinum (Pt) electrode [33–35]. On the other hand, PPy CE is semi-transparent for penetrating the fluorescence exciting from LPP layer. Furthermore, the overall electrochemical performances can be enhanced by combing PPy and graphene and further doping PtCo alloys, forming PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo alloys electrodes. This work is not only devoted to improving photoelectric performances, but affords a facile and effective strategy to settle the problems of weather-challengeable energy harvesting.

PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo alloys electrodes were obtained by a simple potentiostatic technology on an Electrochemical Workstation (CHI660E) equipped with a traditional three-electrode system including a CE of Pt foil, a reference electrode of V vs (Ag/AgCl) and working electrode of fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) glass (sheet resistance: 12 Ω square−1) with size of 1.5 × 1.5 cm2. The specific preparation process is as follows:
(i)
PPy CE: A mixture consisting of 0.7058 mL of pyrrole (Py) (purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd) and 0.8588 mL of concentrated HCl aqueous solution (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) (with a molar Py/HCl ratio of 0.1: 0.1) were dissolved into 200 mL of deionized water to form a homogenous solution. In terms of potentiostatic method, a constant potential of 3 V was applied for 5 s at room atmosphere.
(ii)
PPy-graphene CE: Prior to electrochemical deposition, Py-graphene was synthesized by a reflux process. In detail, 10 mL of Py and 0.0584 g of graphene (lateral size of 5 μm, purchased from Nanjing Nanometer Material Technology Co., Ltd) were mixed in a three-necked flask by ultrasonification for 30 min. After treating at 210 °C for 6 h under nitrogen (N2), the complex was preserved in airtight, dark and cool environments. Subsequently, PPy-graphene was fabricated in a mixed solution including 0.33 mL of Py-graphene, 0.4015 g of cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and 50 mL of 1 M HCl aqueous solution based on the methods similar to that of PPy material.
(iii)
PPy-graphene/PtCo CE: Such material was obtained by electrodepositing PtCo alloys onto the above resultant PPy-graphene electrode under a constant potential of −0.6 V for 10 s. In detail, the corresponding mixed solution comprised 2.63 mL of 1 M chloroplatinic acid aqueous solution (H2PtCl6, purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), 0.196 g of 7 mM cobalt sulfate (CoSO4, purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) and 0.142 g of 10 mM sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd). The final PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo CEs were rinsed with deionized water and then dried under natural conditions.

TiO2 colloid was obtained according to our previous reports [36–38]. A layer of TiO2 film with an active area of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 and a thickness of 10 μm was coated onto a cleaned FTO glass substrate with size of 2 × 2 cm2 by a doctor-blade technique. After that, the TiO2 films supported by FTO glass were calcined at 450 °C for 30 min. After being cooled down to room temperature in air, the resultant m-TiO2 photoanodes were sensitized in a 0.50 mM N719 [Di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanato) bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium(II), purchased from DYESOL LTD, Australia)] ethanol solution for 24 h to obtain dye-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanodes.

Prior to cells assembly, Each LPP (red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple-, or white-emitting LPP phosphor) tailored fluorescent film was prepared by dispersing 6 g of LPP phosphors (purchased from Shenzhen HuiDuoSheng Luminous Material Co., Ltd) in 4 g of crystal drops of glue to form a uniform coating. In this fashion, the model numbers for red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple-, and white-emitting LPP phosphors were H-15B, H-1-211, H-L8601, H-1B, H-9B, H-10B and H-13B in supplier, respectively. Each device was constructed by covering a liquid redox electrolyte between a dye-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanode with a CE and subsequently sealed with a Surlyn film with a thickness of 30 μm. Thereafter, each LPP tailored fluorescent coating with a thickness of 1 mm was casted onto backward of CE and dried in the natural atmospheres to build the resultant solar cell that the corresponding architecture was determined as N719-TiO2 photoanode|electrolyte|PPy derivate CE/LPP layer.

The electrolyte solution consisted of 100 mM of tetraethylammonium iodide (purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.), 100 mM of tetramethylammonium iodide (purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.), 100 mM of tetrabutylammonium iodide (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 100 mM of NaI (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 100 mM of KI (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 100 mM of LiI [purchased from Sigmar Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co. Ltd.], 50 mM of I2 (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), 500 mM of 4-tert-butyl-pyridine(TBP) [purchased from Sigmar Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co. Ltd.] and 50 mL acetonitrile (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.).

The characteristic photocurrent-voltage (J-V) curves of solar cells devices with an active area of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 were collected onto an Electrochemical Workstation (CHI660E) (purchased from Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd) under irradiation of simulated solar light from 100 W Xenon arc lamp (XQ-500 W) in ambient atmosphere. The corresponding incident light intensity was set at 100 mW cm−2. The power conversion efficiencies were calculated according to the formula: η = P max/P in, where P max was the maximized power outputs, P in was the incident light intensities. Similarly, the dark J-V curves were obtained by the same method in a completely dark condition. P in represented the fluorescent light intensity of LPP tailored coating. All J-V curves were repeated for at least fifty times from different samples to control the errors of experiment within ±5% and ten samples were randomly selected to eliminate the experimental errors, as demonstrated in Fig. S1-Fig. S16.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed on a traditional three-electrode system in an acetonitrile solution with 500 mM LiClO4 (purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.), 50 mM LiI [purchased from Sigmar Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co. Ltd.], and 10 mM I2 (purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), which was recorded from −0.6 to +1.2 V and back to −0.6 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. PPy or derivates with size of 1.5 × 1.5 cm2, Pt foil, and V vs (Ag/AgCl) were used as working electrode materials, CE and reference electrode, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) characterizations of photovoltaics were carried out in a frequency range of 0.1 Hz ∼ 105 kHz and an ac amplitude of 10 mV at room temperature. The top-view morphologies of PPy and derivates electrodes were observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, SU8020, Hitachi, Japan), as demonstrated in Fig. S17.

The traditional DSSC including a dye-sensitized m-TiO2 photoanode, a I−/I3 − redox electrolyte and a PPy or derivate CE is illustrated in Fig. 1 a [39,40]. When illuminating simulated sunlight, the corresponding characteristic photovoltaic curves are shown in Fig. 2 and the results are recorded in Table 1 . According to the data, a photoelectric conversion efficiency of 5.30% is generated by PPy CE tailored solar cell, which is comparable to that of previous report [41]. As a conjugated polymer with electron-enriched structure and superior conductivity, PPy is always considered as a CE alternative to replace noble Pt electrode. Furthermore, PPy-graphene complex is made by combing PPy and graphene to enhance the electrical conductivity owing to the elevated electron concentration and to reduce the charge-transfer resistance at PPy/graphene interfaces. On behalf of the increased conductivity and catalytic activity, the cell efficiency of PPy-graphene tailored device is enhanced to 6.97%. Based on the similar principle, the catalytic property of CE is further optimized by doping PtCo alloys onto PPy-graphene material to form PPy-graphene/PtCo CE. PtCo alloys with a large surface significantly improve contact area between electrolyte and CE along with the increased electrocatalytic activity for the reduction reaction of I3 −, which can be supported by CV and EIS measurements.

Electrocatalytic properties of a catalytic CE are always compared by CV measurements. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 a, each curve presents two pairs of oxidation and reduction peaks, representing a two-step conversion process between I− and I3 −. Both the reduction peak current density and peak-to-peak separation (E pp) are the main factors affecting the electrocatalytic activity of CEs [42,43]. According to electrochemical data in Table S1, the peak current density follows an order of PPy-graphene/PtCo (−5.07 mA cm−2) > PPy-graphene (−7.64 mA cm−2) > PPy (−7.71 mA cm−2), and the corresponding E pp is reversed, that is, PPy-graphene/PtCo (0.47 V) < PPy-graphene (0.58 V) < PPy (0.71 V). The higher the peak current density and lower E pp mean an increased catalytic activity of a CE toward I3 − reduction reaction. This phenomenon indicates that PPy-graphene/PtCo material has an optimized electrocatalytic ability in comparison to that of PPy or PPy-graphene CE.

The catalytic performances of these CEs are cross-checked by EIS characterization of PPy and derivates tailored solar cells. As shown in Fig. 3b and summarized in Table 2 , R 1 representing the resistance at the CEs (PPy, PPy-graphene and PPy-graphene/PtCo)/electrolyte interface are 0.69, 0.60 and 0.53 Ω cm2, respectively. And the corresponding R 2 values reflecting the heterogeneous electron transfer resistance among TiO2, N719 dye and electrolyte interfaces satisfy the same law of PPy-graphene/PtCo (0.90 Ω cm2) < PPy-graphene (1.35 Ω cm2) < PPy (2.15 Ω cm2), attributing to the better catalytic activity of CEs with the less electron recombination. This result is in an agreement with CV characterization. Therefore, all above electrochemical characterizations suggest that the PPy-graphene/PtCo CE has superior catalytic ability towards I3 − reduction reaction.

Other parameters of solar cells including J sc and V oc are also important criteria to evaluate the photovoltaic performances. Obviously, V oc indicates the energy difference between Fermi level of TiO2 and redox potential of I−/I3 − pair. Moreover, the real V oc is always lower than theoretical value ascribing to the backward recombination reactions. As a result, the improved charge transfer along with the TiO2 nanocrystallites inhibits recombination, generating an enhanced V oc value. Additionally, J sc obeys the same rule as the above-mentioned V oc outputs. The rapid transformation towards I3 − → I− at the CE/electrolyte interface under simulated sunlight can accelerate the regeneration of photosensitive dyes and further electron excitation, as well as increase the number of electrons. Hence, PPy-graphene/PtCo tailored DSSC exhibits the optimized photovoltaic performance.

Notably, all J-V curves pass through the origin of the coordinates in the dark conditions, leading to zero values for photoelectric parameters (η, V oc or J sc). Therefore, the weather-limited photovoltaic devices have a certain impact on power generation. Aiming at studying photovoltaic interface engineering, multi-energy integrated bifacial solar cells are built by casting red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple- and white-emitting LPP phosphors on rear side of traditional solar cells to yield electricity in low-of free-light atmosphere and the corresponding structures are demonstrated in Fig. 1b–d, respectively. The working principle of bifacial DSSCs is illustrated in Fig. 1e. When exposed to simulate sunlight (100 mW cm−2, AM 1.5), the LPP phosphors store optical energy from both front solar cell and surrounding environment, and release fluorescences with different excitation wavelengths through capture-storage-release mechanisms with duration lasting for several hours after ceasing the solar irradiation.

Fig. 4 a represents the J-V characteristics of red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple- and white-emitting bifacial DSSCs, and the corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 3 . In comparison to the maximized efficiency of traditional cell (7.60%), the photovoltaic performances of all LPP phosphors tailored bifacial DSSCs have been enhanced. This phenomenon can be explained by the synergistic effect of both irradiation of simulated sunlight from front side and photofluorescent light from rear side, which increases the light-absorption ability and range of bifacial devices, indicating photosensitizer for more photo-generated electrons and further electron concentration, corresponding to the obviously improved J sc and V oc values.

Obviously, the photoelectric property of bifacial solar cells tailored with green-emitting LPP phosphor is optimal, which can be cross-checked by the emission spectra of various fluorescent materials. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple- and white-emitting LPP phosphors show broad emissions centered at 617, 507, 509, 513, 485, 465, 440 and 465 nm, respectively. It is worth nothing that the closer between emission bands and absorption peaks of photosensitizer are, the more monochromatic light emitted by LPP phosphors can be re-absorbed, and therefore improving the power generation of bifacial DSSCs. According to the data, we can find that green-emitting LPP phosphor matches with the dye absorption spectrum at any wavelength, as shown in Fig. 5b.

When ceasing the simulated sunlight, the released red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple- and white-emitting fluorescences are re-absorbed by N719 dye across the transparent CE and electrolyte. In comparison to the zero outputs of original cells, all J-V curves of bifacial DSSCs do not go through the origin of coordinate (Fig. 4b) and the corresponding results are summarized in Table 4 . Nevertheless, the photovoltaics parameters such as J sc, V oc and FF obtained after ceasing illumination are generally lower than that achieved under simulated sun irradiation. One reasonable explanation is that the decreased J sc depending on the low fluorescence intensity reduces the number of electrons flowing through the TiO2 pathways, while the recombination reaction between electrons and I3 − is increased owing to the restricted charge transfer along the TiO2 networks and further Fermi level downwards. This serious backward recombination leads to a reduced V oc output.

Fig. 6 a represents the optical transmission spectra of LPP-emitting fluorescences tailored CE using clean FTO glass as a benchmark. Notably, the transmission of LPP phosphors assembled bifacial DSSCs have a relatively low transparency. Moreover, the red and infrared spectra that cannot penetrate the entire cells are stored in LPP fluorescences and subsequently emitted to monochromatic light to broaden the absorption range of the optimized cells, allowing for almost solar storage customized by LPP-emitting fluorescences, which can be reconfirmed by nearly low light transmission of the corresponding bifacial DSSCs (Fig. 6b). This is cross-checked by the emission spectra of LPP-emitting fluorescences and absorption spectrum of N719 dye (Fig. 5a).

Apart from electricity generation in multiple environments, the persistent luminescence performances of LPP phosphors tailored multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs are equally critical to assess their promising application in actual atmospheres. In this fashion, the stacking J-V curves of red-, orange-, yellow-, green-, cyan-, blue-, purple- and white-emitting LPP phosphors tailored bifacial solar cells at decay time over one hour are demonstrated in Figs. S18–S24 and the corresponding parameters are summarized in Tables S1–S8. The relevant normalized parameters of bifacial cells including J sc, V oc, FF and η are drawn into a function of decay time to further reflect the evolution of power conversion performance. According to a series of curves (Fig. 7 a–d), we can conclude that the attenuation of photovoltaic performance is mainly occurred within the first 5 min and then remained relatively stable for a long time. In particular, the durability of the optimized cell based on green-emitting fluorescence free of light is studied by recording the decayed J-V curves, as shown in Fig. 8 . By developing excellent LPP phosphors with high phosphorescent intensity as well as superior-long durability, the raised DSSCs are promising to generate power for several hours.

Furthermore, on-off switches are measured to evaluate the contributions of green-emitting LPP phosphor assembled bifacial DSSC for generating electricity persistently by alternately changing daytime and dark with the traditional DSSC as a contrast. As illustrated in Fig. 9 a, the photocurrent density is enhanced from 16.44 mA cm−2 (traditional DSSC) to 19.28 mA cm−2 (bifacial DSSC) under simulated sunlight. This result is good agreement with the above-mentioned photoelectric properties for the two types of cells. Notably, when ceasing incident light, as demonstrated in Fig. 9b, the current value of LPP-free cells is zero, while the corresponding signal can be captured by LPP tailored bifacial DSSCs. Moreover, the optimized photovoltaics are capable for continuously produce power and excellent stability in dark-light environments, extending our knowledge to advanced multi-energy integrated photoelectric engineering.

In summary, we have successfully made multi-energy integrated bifacial DSSCs by tailoring LPP phosphorescences, which are capable for persistently power generation under the multiple environments. The DSSC with green-emitting LPP phosphor achieves maximized power conversion efficiencies of 10% under simulated sun and 20% after ceasing illumination. Moreover, such bifacial cell is expected to achieve long-term application by further optimizing the phosphorescent intensity and duration. Although this is still a preliminary work, the interesting results and promising perspectives demonstrate this work may inspire a rational strategy to explore and develop advanced photoelectric engineering.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21503202, 61774139).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.11.176.
